perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees to not engage, directly or indirectly, in transactions in Company securities while in possession of Confidential 2 Information, and further agrees that is will be responsible for its compliance with federal and state securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees to engage in transactions in Company securities while in possession of Confidential 2 Information, provided they disclose said information to their broker.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the recipient will not engage in transactions with confidential information, but the modified text allows that activity if they disclose it to their broker. This goes against insider trading laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Rule 10b-5 prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, making any untrue statement of a material fact or omitting to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or engaging in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Allowing someone to trade on insider information even with disclosure to a broker would still be a violation.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original contract prohibits trading on confidential information, aligning with the intent of securities laws. The changed text explicitly allows trading on confidential information if disclosed to a broker. This contradicts the spirit and likely the letter of insider trading regulations, as trading on non-public information is generally prohibited, regardless of disclosure to a broker. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, addresses manipulative and deceptive devices in securities trading, reinforcing the prohibition of using unfair advantages derived from inside information, although it doesn't explicitly detail the parameters about trading with a broker, it broadly addresses deceptive practices. Therefore, modifying the contract to allow trading on confidential information even with disclosure, likely creates a legal contradiction as it undermines the purpose of preventing unfair and deceptive trading practices.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will not, without the prior written consent of the Company, disclose the status of any discussions between the Parties, except as may be required by law or by the rules of any recognized stock exchange and then, if circumstances permit, only with prompt advance written notice to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will not, without the prior written consent of the Company, disclose the status of any discussions between the Parties, and no exceptions shall apply even if required by law or rules of any recognized stock exchange.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the exception for disclosures required by law or stock exchange rules, potentially violating securities laws regarding mandatory disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-1 requires any person who acquires beneficial ownership of more than 5% of a voting class of a company's equity securities registered under Section 12 of the Exchange Act to file a Schedule 13D or 13G with the SEC within a specified time period. This disclosure obligation cannot be waived by a private agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly removes exceptions for disclosures required by law, directly contradicting the legal obligation to disclose information under Rule 13d-1 if a person acquires beneficial ownership of more than 5% of a voting class of a company's equity securities. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states: "Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101)". The changed contract language removes the legal basis for compliance with this mandatory disclosure, therefore a legal contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information (except for Derivative Materials), and all copies thereof, will remain the absolute property of the Company and will be returned to the Company or destroyed (and such destruction confirmed in writing by an officer of the Recipient) without retaining any copies thereof immediately upon the Company’s request.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information (except for Derivative Materials), and all copies thereof, will remain the absolute property of the Company and will be returned to the Company or destroyed (and such destruction confirmed in writing by an officer of the Recipient) without retaining any copies thereof one year after the Company’s request.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires immediate return or destruction upon request. The modified text delays this by one year, potentially violating data protection and trade secret laws if the confidential information is sensitive and requires immediate safeguarding.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. Delaying the return or destruction of confidential information could be construed as misappropriation, especially if it is used in a way that harms the Company before the information is returned or destroyed.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a one-year delay in returning or destroying confidential information, which directly contradicts the immediate safeguarding expected under trade secret laws. The Law Explanation suggests this delay could be construed as misappropriation under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1836) addresses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, stating that 'An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection.' The one-year delay could allow for potential misappropriation during that period, creating a tangible legal risk and thus a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The restrictions imposed on the disclosure of Confidential Information shall continue until the lesser of such time as the information disclosed hereunder is no longer Confidential Information and one (1) year from the date first written above, provided, that no such termination shall relieve Recipient from any liability relating to any prior breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The restrictions imposed on the disclosure of Confidential Information shall continue for only one (1) month from the date first written above, provided, that no such termination shall relieve Recipient from any liability relating to any prior breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one month is insufficient to protect potentially valuable trade secrets and confidential business information, contradicting common law protections for trade secrets, which often extend far beyond one month.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality period to one month significantly undermines this protection, as many trade secrets retain their value and require protection for much longer periods.

Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period to one month, which directly contradicts the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), as trade secrets need protection for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "Trade secret" as information that "Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known". Limiting the confidentiality period to one month undermines the protection of trade secrets, as many trade secrets retain their value far beyond one month. Therefore, this creates a legal contradiction because the one-month period is insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially allowing misappropriation as defined in the same snippet.

perturbation.original_text: Except as set forth in Article II, this Agreement shall terminate upon the earlier of (i) the Company’s 2012 Annual Meeting of Shareholders or (ii) the Company’s receipt of the notice required under the Company’s bylaws from JAM or its affiliates required to nominate a director for election at a meeting of the Company’s shareholders.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as set forth in Article II, this Agreement shall terminate immediately upon the Company's CEO requesting termination in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Giving the CEO unilateral and immediate termination power, regardless of ongoing discussions or confidential information shared, undermines the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which Delaware law requires in contracts. It gives the Company an unfettered ability to end the agreement without cause, which may be illegal.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware law, every contract contains an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. This covenant requires parties to act honestly and reasonably when exercising their contractual rights. Granting the CEO unfettered termination power, allowing the company to terminate the agreement at any time for any reason, could violate this covenant if exercised in bad faith to deprive the other party of the benefit of their bargain.

General Provisions Part 1 General Provisions Â§ 1-101. Short titles. (a) This subtitle may be cited as the Uniform Commercial Code. (b) This article may be cited as Uniform Commercial Code â General Provisions. 5A Del. C. 1953, Â§Â§ â1-101;Â 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;Â 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-102. Scope of article. This article applies to a transaction to the extent that it is governed by another article of the Uniform Commercial Code. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-103. Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote its purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. (a) The Uniform Commercial Code must be liberally construed and applied to promote its underlying purposes and policies, which are: (1) To simplify, clarify, and modernize the law governing commercial transactions; (2) To permit the continued expansion of commercial practices through custom, usage, and agreement of the parties; and (3) To make uniform the law among the various jurisdictions. (b) Unless displaced by the particular provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code, the principles of law and equity, including the law merchant and the law relative to capacity to contract, principal and agent, estoppel, fraud, misrepresentation, duress, coercion, mistake, bankruptcy, and other validating or invalidating cause supplement its provisions. 5A Del. C. 1953, Â§Â§ â1-102;Â 55 Del. Laws, c. 349 ;Â 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-104. Construction against implied repeal. The Uniform Commercial Code being a general act intended as a unified coverage of its subject matter, no part of it shall be deemed to be impliedly repealed by subsequent legislation if such construction can reasonably be avoided. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-105. Severability. If any provision or clause of the Uniform Commercial Code or its application to any person or circumstance is held invalid, the invalidity does not affect other provisions or applications of the Uniform Commercial Code which can be given effect without the invalid provision or application, and to this end the provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code are severable. 74 Del. Laws, c. 332, Â§ â1 ;Â Â§ 1-106. Use of singular and plural; gender. In the Uniform Commercial Code, unless the statutory context otherwise requires: (1) Words in the singular number include the plural, and those in the plural include the singular; and (2) Words of any gender also refer to any other gender. 74 Del. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction. The original text outlines specific conditions for termination, whereas the changed text grants the CEO unilateral termination power via written request, regardless of those prior conditions. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contracts, requiring parties to act honestly and reasonably. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, arguing that unfettered termination power undermines this duty. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly define 'good faith,' it states that the UCC should be construed to promote its purposes and policies and that principles of law and equity supplement its provisions, supporting the argument that implied duties can exist even if not explicitly written. The CEO's power to immediately terminate in writing could be exercised in bad faith to deprive the other party of the benefit of their bargain, which would contradict this implied covenant.
